title: Indigo by Louis Fischer
subject: English

notes.heading: Theme
notes.text: First instance of Satyagraha in India. The chapter highlights self-reliance, courage, non-violence, and leadership that empowers the poor.

summary:
This chapter records Mahatma Gandhi’s first major political movement in India, the Champaran Satyagraha of 1917. It began when Rajkumar Shukla, an illiterate but determined peasant from Champaran, met Gandhi at the 1916 Lucknow Congress. Shukla wanted Gandhi to visit Champaran to fight the injustice faced by indigo sharecroppers. British landlords forced peasants to plant indigo on 15 percent of their land and surrender the entire harvest as rent. This was the tinkathia system.

When synthetic indigo was invented in Germany, the price of natural indigo fell. Landlords then asked peasants for compensation to release them from the agreement. Many peasants signed, but some refused. Gandhi went to Champaran despite being told it was impossible. British officials ordered him to leave, but he disobeyed and was summoned to court. Thousands of peasants demonstrated outside. The government was baffled and dropped the case. Civil disobedience had won.

Gandhi and his team investigated and recorded testimonies from thousands of peasants. The British appointed an official inquiry committee with Gandhi as a member. The landlords agreed to refund 25 percent of the money. Gandhi accepted because the amount was not important; the real victory was that landlords had to surrender part of their money and prestige. The peasants learned that they had rights and courage.

Gandhi stayed to work on social problems too. He opened schools, taught sanitation, and worked for health and education. The Champaran episode was a turning point because it showed that the British could be challenged and that India’s poor had to be included in the freedom struggle.
